subject: math
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Understand the definition of logarithms as the inverse of indices
Convert between index form and logarithm form
Use logarithm tables to find logarithms of numbers
Use antilogarithm tables to find numbers from their logarithms
Use logarithm tables to multiply and divide numbers

sections.heading: Logarithms of Numbers to Base 10
sections.content: The logarithm of a number is the power to which the base must be raised to give that number. In general: if y = nˣ, then x = log_n(y). For base 10: if y = 10ˣ, then x = log₁₀(y). For example, log₁₀(1000) = 3 since 10³ = 1000; log₁₀(100) = 2 since 10² = 100. Converting to log form: 2⁻⁶ = 1/64 becomes log₂(1/64) = −6; 3⁵ = 243 becomes log₃(243) = 5; 10⁴ = 10000 becomes log₁₀(10000) = 4. The integer part of log₁₀(N) equals (number of digits left of decimal point) − 1. The decimal part (mantissa) is found from log tables.

sections.heading: Using Logarithm Tables and Antilogarithm Tables
sections.content: To find the logarithm of a number: (1) write it in standard form, (2) use the table to find the mantissa, (3) add the characteristic (integer part). For example, log₁₀(3900) = log₁₀(3.9 × 10³) = 0.5911 + 3 = 3.5911. The antilogarithm is the inverse: to find a number whose log is 2.7547, look up 0.7547 in the antilog table to get 5684, then apply the characteristic 2 to get 568.4.

sections.heading: Multiplication and Division Using Logarithm Tables
sections.content: To multiply using logarithms: log(A × B) = log A + log B. To divide: log(A ÷ B) = log A − log B. Then find the antilog of the result. For example, to evaluate 4627 × 29.3: log 4627 = 3.6653; log 29.3 = 1.4669; sum = 5.1322; antilog = 135600. To evaluate 819.8 ÷ 3.905: log 819.8 = 2.9137; log 3.905 = 0.5916; difference = 2.3221; antilog = 209.9.
